1:1: Dhṛtarāṣṭra, the blind king, was asking his secretary Sañjaya about the situation on the battlefield of Kurukṣetra. The word dharma-kṣetre (the place of religion) is significant because, on the battlefield of Kurukṣetra, the Supreme Personality of Godhead was present on the side of Arjuna. Dhṛtarāṣṭra was highly fearful about the influence of the holy place on the outcome of the battle. He knew very well that this would influence Arjuna and the sons of Pāṇḍu favorably, because by nature they were all virtuous.
5:2: In this verse, the Lord is resolving Arjuna's confusion regarding the superiority of work in devotion over renunciation of work. Although both the path of sannyāsa (renunciation material activities) and the path of karma-yoga (working in devotion) eventually lead the practitioner to the supreme destination, karma-yoga is superior. Why? Because without purification of the heart, mere renunciation of activities is completely useless and causes distress. A person acting in Kṛṣṇa consciousness (karma-yoga) is automatically a true sannyāsī because he does not claim anything as his own.
